text: Hey guys, welcome to This is My Architecture. My name is Athanu Roy and today I have with me
Faneesh Gururaj, VP Engineering at Redbus. Hey Faneesh, good to see you and thanks for joining
us. Thanks Athanu, thanks for having me. Good to see you too. So Faneesh, for folks who are not
aware about Redbus, why don't you talk a little about what you guys do? Redbus is a classic
marketplace model where we actually connect the bus customers who are trying to do the bus booking
with the bus operators. We are actually operational in five countries outside India and in terms of
the bus booking experience we try to give a very good information right from the time they are
intending to book to the time they are actually traveling and finally do the actual rating and
review of that bus thereby closing the loop. So as an user of Redbus what what strikes me is the way
you guys have made it easier for us to provide feedback about buses and services which helps
other users in decision making while they are booking. So talk us a little bit through that journey.
UGC what we call as user generated content at Redbus is a key differentiator to us. As we scaled into
different things what we saw was the users were giving a lot of information about the particular
bus journey they had. They gave it in the form of an unstructured text which we used to generate
different specific content like bus being punctual, bus having a late boarding time etc. These form the
tags and as things scaled to do this manually became a problem and as things scaled we had to look at other
alternatives. So this is where we used ML as well as SageMaker's pipeline to kind of make this entire
process efficient to generate these dynamic tags. Understood. So let's look at your architecture bit.
Sure. So the users come on to the channel and they give a specific review to the actual SKU that they
traveled against an endpoint. SKU in Redbus terminology means the user who has taken a specific route to
actually complete his journey and give the feedback. This data now gets stored into our Arango cluster
where it is saved in a more unstructured way. Okay interesting choice. So why Arango particularly?
Arango is a classical graph data store. We wanted to get a graphical relationship between the users who
traveled and gave the reviews against a specific bus operator they actually traveled on and the actual
review they gave. So these three different kinds of data sets we wanted to query very efficiently and
graph was a good choice. To make your search more faster and all. Yes. Give me a sense of the kind
of algos you are using to build this dynamic tags. Sure. So we use a combination of manual curation and
k-means to actually label the actual training data and this label training data is now used by the random
forest classifier to actually generate the dynamic tags. Okay. So you are using SageMaker's bring your own
algo feature to bring the random forest into SageMaker. That's correct. So we use a scikit-learn library
on which the actual random forest algo is built. Okay. And using the bring your own algo we actually
import that model into SageMaker. Okay. And then how do you do the training? So what we do is we make use
of the managed training module that is available in SageMaker to get the training done and that data is again
saved back into our Arango store. Okay. It looks like from your use case you need to infer your models
more in a batch mode kind of requirement. Is that correct? Yes. So the batch transform that is available
within the SageMaker comes handy here. As and when the new content is coming into our Arango store,
we run dedicated batch cron jobs which takes in the new content and applies the necessary model through
which we actually generate the dynamic tags. That again gets saved back into our Arango store. Okay.
Understood. And now the big question, how do you manage to keep your models accurate?
That is one of the biggest challenge and here SageMaker's HPO comes handy. Okay. For example,
for our random forest algo, there are a bunch of hyper parameters that we can actually optimize on
which helps us in ensuring better accuracy. Rather than choosing a very random value to get this optimization
going, we use hyper parameter optimization engine to get the right accurate data. And without HPO,
we had to do lot of manual jobs which varied over different cases and that took a long time.
Probably we cut short from a week to a day using this process. That is awesome. And give me a sense of
sense of the scale that you deal with while building running your training jobs. So, for this use case,
especially when we had to train for the first time, we had close to 30 to 35 million reviews. To get that
into the actual labeled data, we ran the entire job and that entire corpus of data on SageMaker and it
scaled beautifully. 30 million is quite a lot. Yes. And given this scale and you already mentioned
random forest and k-means. So, I am just wondering, did you look at the SageMaker built-in algos particularly
considering they are single pass, faster and highly scalable? Yes, we have looked at that and these
are some things that we are also using for our new use cases. Ah, okay. So, what's the future like?
What all other use cases are you working on now? So, we are doing a bunch of things right from dynamic
pricing, fraud detection, also building a chat bot to help the post customer experience. And in all these
cases, we wanted good accuracy at scale and for large data. And I think SageMaker will definitely help us
achieve these objectives. Awesome man. All the best for Nish and thanks again for joining us.
Yeah. And thanks to you for watching This is My Architecture.
